pattern: Because x OR (x + 1) always inherits its trailing run of 1-bits from the position where the increment's carry stops, the smallest valid x is the target value with exactly one bit -- the one just below that run -- cleared.
hints: The bitwise OR of any x and x + 1 always has its lowest bit set, because incrementing always flips at least the lowest bit -- so a value that is even can never be produced this way.

Incrementing x turns its trailing run of 1-bits into 0s and sets the next bit above that run to 1; that means the OR of x and x + 1 always has its own trailing run of 1-bits reaching one bit further than x's does.

The minimal x is obtained by using the longest possible trailing run of 1-bits already present in the target odd number: subtract, from that number, one power of two -- the power corresponding to the position just below that whole run.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, mix of impossible and constructible values
statement example, all odd values with varying trailing-run lengths
the only even prime, always impossible
value whose binary form is a single trailing run of ones (like 2^k - 1)
a prime just one more than a power of two, trailing run length one
several evens and odds mixed together

stress.description: random odd primes drawn by trial division, occasional even prime 2 mixed in

complexity.time: O(n) with O(1) bit tricks per element
